title: Cloudflare Gateway (SWG) DNS and HTTP Filtering
spl:
index=proxy sourcetype="cloudflare:gateway" earliest=-7d
| where action IN ("block", "isolate")
| stats count by policy_name, category, action, user_email
| sort -count
description: Cloudflare Gateway provides DNS-layer and HTTP-layer filtering for all users. DNS blocks stop threats before connections are established; HTTP inspection enforces content and application policies.
value: Security operations teams analyze Cloudflare Gateway DNS and HTTP filtering events, leveraging DNS-layer blocks as the earliest threat interception point to detect compromised endpoints calling C2, and HTTP-layer blocks for content-based policy enforcement.
implementation: Configure Logpush for Gateway logs. Map categories to your acceptable-use policy. Alert on DNS blocks for known-malicious categories. Report on filtering effectiveness and policy coverage.
visualization: Bar chart (blocks by category), Table (top blocked domains), Line chart (daily blocks), Pie chart (DNS vs HTTP blocks).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.user, All_Traffic.action, All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Cloudflare Gateway logs via Logpush in `index=cloudflare` with `sourcetype=cloudflare:logpush:gateway_dns` and `sourcetype=cloudflare:logpush:gateway_http`. Key fields (DNS): `QueryName`, `QueryType`, `PolicyName`, `Action` (allow/block/override), `DeviceName`, `Email`. Key fields (HTTP): `URL`, `Action`, `PolicyName`, `ContentCategory`, `SecurityCategory`.
• Cloudflare Gateway combines DNS filtering (block domains before connection) and HTTP inspection (inspect content after TLS decryption). DNS filtering is lighter but less granular; HTTP inspection sees URLs and content.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Configure Gateway Logpush: Cloudflare Dashboard > Zero Trust > Logs > Logpush > create jobs for Gateway DNS and Gateway HTTP datasets.

Verify:
```spl
(index=cloudflare sourcetype="cloudflare:logpush:gateway_dns") OR (index=cloudflare sourcetype="cloudflare:logpush:gateway_http")
| stats count by sourcetype, Action
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Gateway filtering overview:
```spl
index=cloudflare sourcetype="cloudflare:logpush:gateway_dns" Action=block earliest=-7d
| timechart span=1d count by SecurityCategory limit=10
```

Understanding this SPL: Trends DNS blocks by Cloudflare security category. 'Malware', 'Phishing', and 'Command & Control' are the critical categories. DNS blocks are the earliest interception — they prevent even the initial connection. A spike indicates active campaigns. Persistent blocks from the same device suggest a compromised endpoint.

HTTP inspection blocks:
```spl
index=cloudflare sourcetype="cloudflare:logpush:gateway_http" Action=block earliest=-24h
| stats count by ContentCategory, SecurityCategory, Email
| sort - count
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Resolve a known-blocked domain. Verify DNS block.
(b) In Cloudflare: Zero Trust > Logs > Gateway.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — DNS block trend. Row 2 — HTTP inspection blocks. Row 3 — C2/Malware blocks by device.
Alert: Immediate on C2/Malware DNS blocks from internal devices.

Runbook:
1. **C2 DNS block** — Device compromised. Isolate and investigate.
2. **Phishing DNS spike** — Active campaign. Alert users.
3. **HTTP block on legit site** — Miscategorization. Submit for review.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No Gateway DNS data** — Logpush for Gateway DNS may not be enabled.
• **No HTTP data** — HTTP inspection requires TLS decryption to be enabled.
grandmaExplanation: Cloudflare blocks dangerous websites at two levels — first it prevents your computer from even looking up the address (DNS), and then it inspects the actual content. We monitor both levels because the DNS blocks catch threats at the earliest possible moment, before any data is exchanged.
hardware: Cloudflare Zero Trust (cloud), Cloudflare WARP client
